sentences.text: I was born on the 28th of December, 1926, in a village called Alsovalko.
A-L-S-O--
--dash [SIGHS] V-A-L-K-O.
In Romania.
It has a Romanian name of Valcau de Jos.
No.
Just those two names.
I was-- I had a very happy childhood.
I was free.
We had close-by mountains, winter sports, river in, in the end of the backyard, big garden with-- full of fruit trees.
And there was no choice of schools, only public school.
And when I-- in 1938, my parents decided to send me to a Jewish high school.
It was about 110 kilometers away.
Severe winters, yes.
Snow.
The river was completely frozen over.
You couldn't just walk through it.
Skate on the river, yeah--
--until--
Really, we had to clean the snow.
And some [? did ?] without skates, just-- they had to-- like horseshoes.
And, and the back of the heel, it just went through the ice.
It was like a mirror.
No, the ice was about 8 or-- 8 or 10 inches deep, frozen.
Ah, yes.
All the children.
[LAUGHS]
Well, the snow lasted usually-- it fall in the beginning of December, or during December.
And it lasted till end of March.
Yeah, I did.
Proper skis, yeah.
No.
No.
Actually, I, I did ski.
I took always a friend home from school--
--who came in the winter holidays, which we had only two or three weeks, Christmastime.
And always, someone, someone from my class accompanied me.
So the two of us, we went skiing.
Because nobody had skis, only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call that, sledge?
Yeah.
Sledges everybody had, but not ski.
I had the skis from-- with me in town.
No.
I had my own.
The-- in high school-- when I went to high school, we-- I was a member of a club which owned a hut in the mountains.
So we could use that free of charge as-- about 10 days a year.
Well, I, I skied even after the war, but not now.
Not anymore.
Sometimes, I think you can't forget it.
If I would go, I would succeed.
I might fall a few times, but that's all.
I, I did try ice skating in Sydney-- [LAUGHS]
--which-- I, I went all right.
Still, you don't forget.
It's like bicycle riding.
[LAUGHS]
Correct.
They, they were mostly-- a majority were Romanian children, about 60%.
And about, oh, could be 35% were Hungarian children.
And would be about two, two or three Jewish kids local.
That's all.
About 12 families-- 12 to 15.
Yes, we had a synagogue.
Yeah, [? we were. ?]
Yeah, special-- that, that was next door to our place.
Yeah, next door to our house.
No.
We, we only had visiting rabbis from other places.
Sometimes on other occasions, too.
When you had a, a brit or wedding, they came.
Or if somebody died, they, they, they came to officiate.
They come from-- there were three places.
One was Kraszna.
And one was--
K-R-A-S-Z-N-A.
They had a rabbi there.
And then there, there was a very large village with more Jewish people called Nagyfalu--
--which was only--
--10 Kilometers.
Their-- Nagyfalu means, in English, 'big village'--
--translated.
But that was the name of it.
They had a permanent rabbi.
And then they had the rabbi in Somlyo.
That was [INAUDIBLE], 18 kilometers away.
S-O-M-L-Y-O.
By horse.
--horse carriage.
Horse-drawn carriage, yeah.
Just the wagon, not closed.
Open wagon.
Everything.
Snow, we had sledge--
Horse--
--horse-drawn sledge.
Yeah.
We had a cheder.
I can't recall the name.
But the cheder was in our place.
We, we had--
Not our house.
We had a house in, in our backyard, which was transformed from-- it was originally agricultural machinery in part of it, and even livestock in the other end.
And then two rooms were kept, which was the closest to our house.
The end of that, that building was kept for cheder.
And we had our classes practically every day.
Oh, about six or eight children.
No.
The, the cheder teacher, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] actually, he, he came from somewhere else.
But then he resided here permanently for years.
In, in the village, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's right.
In the afternoon, yeah.
In high school?
Well, my parents stayed all the time there.
I went to the high school 110, 120 kilometers away.
That was a place-- Oradea.
And--
O-R-- O-R-D-E-A.
And that was a city of about 60,000 people.
And it had two Jewish schools.
One was an Orthodox religious school, which had only four classes.
And one was a Reformed school, where I went, which had eight classes.
So they didn't want to put me in the religious one because I had to come over anyway to this one in the fifth one.
Up to certain grade.
And if they wanted to continue, they had to come over to us.
And--
They weren't too happy about it because we were not religious in that school.
Yes.
Yeah.
My father had-- well, we-- he inherited some land.
And--
Some were close.
Some were not close.
And he had, say-- my grandfather had spirit factory.
And he was also--
Well, they call it spirit.
Plums and--
--like a normal spirit, 96 degree-- [LAUGHTER]
A proper spirit.
And he also had cattle for, for feeding-- for slaughter, but he-- they were using from the mills.
They, they had flour mill, oil, oil mill, and the spirits.
No, that was part of the other family.
So they, they were feeding these with grain, the, the animals.
Yeah, from the-- then that was in-- after Second World War, in, in 1928, they--
After the First World War--
--they, they-- these lands were taken away.
And they were given some coupons, which wasn't worth anything.
And what was a lot people-- it was about-- we were left about 40 acres, something like that.
Yeah.
Well, my father owned part of it because he kept selling it all the time.
And then in the last three years, actually, he, he-- we had very little left, no more machinery.
And he had a, a shop-- he opened a shop in the same building, which sold groceries and hardware.
I, I really-- I really don't know what's--
Well, those 40 were not enough.
You could produce enough for yourself, but not much to sell.
But the grandfather had much more.
And that was taken away.
So--
No food.
No food, except essentials of grocery, like salt, sugar--
--flours, that sort of thing.
But it mainly were tools.
And I can remember they did bartering.
Some, some of the people came from distant villages, hours to buy sugar or salt.
And they didn't have money.
They bartered eggs.
Fresh eggs or other products they produced, and they wanted sugar instead.
So you just had to calculate how many-- what was the price of the egg, and how much sugar they can buy for so many eggs.
[LAUGHS] That went on for years like that.
Yes, I remember it.
My father's hobby was-- he, he did woodworking, his hobby.
And I'm doing it, too.
I remember the last three years during the Second World War, he actually leased the building, which had the cheder and the livestock and the [INAUDIBLE] machinery.
He leased it to a Turkish company, who employed Ukrainian-Russian people from Bessarabia.
And they produced dried prunes from fresh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call that prunes-- Angelina.
From Angelina plums, the blue ones, which you eat.
And they even made delicacies, I remember.
They-- after a day, it, it was put into, into this huge stove, which were in the ground, 2-meter-diameter pipes running in-- and above were a structure of timber, with trays in it and doors.
Each door had about five trays, and were five, five-- 3 times 5 vertically and all along, could have been about 25 feet each.
So a lot of plums went in there.
And those plums-- you, you could-- after a day, 24 hours, you could take the plums out, and squeeze the seed out through one end of it, and replace it with, with a walnut-- Wow.
--with a walnut seed and keep drying it further.
I shouldn't have told you.
This is trade secrets.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, in the back.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they, they kept it till, till the-- we were deported from that place.
That was seasonal because they only used it for about three months in a year.
Oh, in the whole area.
People came with the plums in horse-drawn carriages.
And they were selling them to this company.
They went, went onto the scale with the livestock, with the carriage, and then when empty.
And they just deducted the difference-- [LAUGHTER] --and paid for, for whatever was the weight of a plum.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was a very nice lady.
Hungarian name was Lenke.
And she-- her Hebrew name was Leah.
Is Steinberger.
And she come from an area another 100 kilometers away from a different direction, north from our place.
Yes.
Halmeu in the Romanian language, or Halmi in Hungarian.
H-A-L-M-I.
And that was a little township close to Szatmar, which was a Jewish center.
Yeah.
Very religious.
Yes.
She, she read a lot.
And books had to be brought from 18 kilometers away.
No, from the township, from Somlyo.
There was a library there.
And you, you could get five-- four or five books at a time from--
And she got them on a regular basis.
--public library.
Yeah.
Besides reading?
She had a household.
She helped in the shop.
And she was painting.
Yeah.
Only one painting I have, which-- a still life.
An oil painting she painted in 1917.
No, day-- daytime.
Yeah.
She painted on natural light only.
Because we, we didn't have electricity in this place.
No running water.
From a well.
I could carry it, for drinking water.
And, and no hot water, of course, in the house, either.
No hot water.
There was just a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bath, which was heated by steam from the flour mill, which was driven by steam.
And the, the flour mill actually-- the mill itself was driven by water.
But next to it was another mill for sunflowers--
--for oil.
That was driven by steam.
And they used the skin of the sunflower to fire the steam engine.
Part of the-- of the skin of, of, of the shell--
--went into a separate chamber.
And they just used shovels to put it on, on the fire, mixed with timber.
[LAUGHS] And the, the-- that bath was made-- you went down steps, deep, level with that small river, which was driven the-- which was driving the flour mills.
So you had fresh water from that river.
This was-- diverted part of the river only, enough to, to drive two big--
Yeah, Beretyo, of course.
B-E-R-E-T-Y-O.
And it was a good, good river, full of fish.
And people even drank from the river who didn't have a well or they had--
No.
The, the diverted part was fast.
And that, that couldn't freeze from the speed of, of it.
But the main river was frozen over, which it was slow in parts.
In certain places where it was fast, it couldn't freeze even that--
--where it was rapids.
But you could see ice coming down from the big timber, which were-- was catching the water and driving the mill.
It, it was a wheel, maybe four meter in diameter and about one meter wide.
And the river-- the-- when the frost came and part of it was frozen like candles sticking on the side.
So it looked like a stalagmite in a-- in a cave.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Oh, I, I got one, one-- two friends still alive.
And they're living in Israel.
Yeah.
And--
Yes.
They were with me in concentration camps.
We were liberated together, same carriage.
Not really.
I want-- I wanted to change.
The life in a, in bigger city, I like it.
I never had brothers and sisters.
Oh yeah.
I lived in Oradea, in my aunt's place.
My mother's sister.
I stayed with her.
She never had children.
Six years, and after the war again.
Yes.
Yeah.
It was in-- that was-- 15 years old.
Yeah.
So I, I was born in '26, and the war broke out in 1940.
The war with Russia broke out '41.
So with France, that was a quick-- a quick work over, [LAUGHS] practically.
We, we met groups of-- not in my village, but in Oradea, where, where I went to high school.
I could remember on Fridays, they used to have transports coming from Vienna.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- one of the large bakeries used to bake extra bread and bring them out to the station.
There was Jewish people who went to Palestine.
Traveling on trains going towards the Black Sea Port.
They had--
Some of them, yeah.
Some of them spoke Hungarian.
They were Austrian Jews.
Yeah.
To the Black Sea.
And then they, they had passports.
And, and some had, of course, entry permits, British entry permits to Palestine.
I think it must have been '42-- '41, '42.
Well, I was in a Jewish school.
And other schools had a very-- in the same town, we had about six high schools, mixed.
And some were co-educational, and some weren't.
Some were Romanian language.
Some were-- had Hungarian language.
And many Jewish people had two schools.
And they went to those two schools as--
One, one was an Orthodox high school, and one was Reformist.
They had a choice of two schools.
Four, four boys' schools.
And then they had separate girls' schools, as well.
There were very many schools.
Some were state schools.
Some were-- belonged to different churches.
Yeah.
Not--
Not so much of Europe.
Only when the Germans entered Hungary, things changed.
Until then--
There was effects in the war, because we had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anti-Semitism was growing, and--
Well, the-- high schools, you couldn't feel it so much.
But universities was limited.
Jewish people could only attend, I think, 6% of the total.
That was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the war, even.
The Hungarians introduced that.
Yeah.
Not-- we didn't, we didn't sense-- not all the teachers were Jewish.
We had non-Jewish teachers as well.
Well, we had Romanian teachers, and one Hungarian teacher for gymnastics and physical culture, and a Romanian one for music.
All the rest were Jewish teachers.
In 1938, it was Romanian.
In 1940, it became Hungarian.
And parts of the curriculum which we went through in 1938, we had to repeat three, four years later because it was a different curriculum under Hungarian system, especially languages.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
I do.
My parents spoke Hungarian.
They know, but they couldn't-- they didn't speak properly.
Correct.
It's common.
You had-- you had certain villages that were pure Hungarian population in Romania.
It's even now the same.
They've got 2 and 1/2 million Hungarians living there today.
Yes, I do.
First, we had some fights in the street, daytime.
It was--
Personally and others from the classroom, from school.
And we had uniforms.
And marked-- we had distinguished caps, each school.
So if, if one of us was attacked because he went in a street-- side street-- on his own, and he had other high school students from another school who were beating him up.
Or if we were more than one, then they wouldn't touch.
[LAUGHS] So they picked on a single student.
They picked on, on single ones, which they were sure they could beat him.
So it was non-Jewish children walking the street.
They'd pick on a Jewish child walking alone.
And they were also high school students, mainly the same age.
Yeah, there were quite a few-- lots of fights.
That happened, too.
It happened--
--because they-- someone beat his friend up a few days earlier.
So he, he just knew who he was, and he was just talking to him.
It happened.
Yes.
They, they also-- see, in the middle of the Second World War, our school was requisitioned by the government and made into a military hospital, with wounded Hungarian soldiers from the front.
We were put into a girls' school for afternoon shift.
So they finished 1 o'clock or 2:00.
And we started from 2:00 till 7:00 at night.
It was quite fun because you could leave notes-- [LAUGHTER] --and meet the girl who was sitting in the same bench in the morning.
[LAUGHTER]
And we met some girls like that, that way, too.
And that went on for how long?
That, that went on till, till we were deported.
We, we never got our school building back--
--because the war was still going.
And the-- they were-- they're having more and more wounded people from the front line.
That's correct.
In 1940, it became Hungary.
Not in Transylvania.
It became Hungary.
That's right.
Part of, of Hungary.
Well, when the, when the war broke out with the Soviet Union, then--
I remember, actually, the days when German troops moved down through our township by train and, and mechanized units to-- before they attacked Greece and Yugoslavia.
They built up their forces close to the-- those borders.
They went through Hungary and Romania.
Yes.
And some of them stopped for two, three hours in, in the station.
And some of these German soldiers came out and bought goods.
Some even signed coupons, hand-written coupons by themselves, that Hitler would pay for it.
[LAUGHTER]
And they didn't want to-- any, any Jewish shop who, who they entered just kept quiet and were happy to get rid of them.
